manual_audit_notes.llm_analysis_excerpt: The provided evidence is a support letter dated June 13, 2022, from various advocacy organizations to the San Jose City Council. It urges the adoption of Item 10.3, a Comprehensive Parking Policy and TDM Ordinance, scheduled for a vote on June 14, 2022. The letter argues that eliminating parking minimums and shifting to market-based parking supply will reduce greenhouse gas emissions, lower housing costs, and support climate neutrality goals.

requested_policy_scope.analysis_question: Capture authoritative San Jose parking policy evidence including policy action, enforcement scope, and effective dates.
requested_policy_scope.search_query: San Jose parking minimums ordinance policy action city council

fail_closed_reasons: analysis_sufficiency:Insufficient. The evidence is a letter advocating for the policy prior to a vote (June 14, 2022) rather than the enacted legislation itself. Consequently, it lacks the final authoritative policy text, specific enforcement scope details, and the actual effective dates of the ordinance.

key_points: The City Council was scheduled to vote on adopting a Comprehensive Parking Policy and TDM Ordinance on June 14, 2022.
Advocacy groups urge the elimination of parking minimums to base car storage on market demand instead of mandates.
The policy aims to support San Jose's goal of becoming carbon neutral by 2030.
Cited evidence suggests parking requirements increase housing costs by 17% for renter households.
The document is a letter of advocacy and does not contain the text of the adopted ordinance or enforcement details.
analysis.sufficiency_state: Insufficient. The evidence is a letter advocating for the policy prior to a vote (June 14, 2022) rather than the enacted legislation itself. Consequently, it lacks the final authoritative policy text, specific enforcement scope details, and the actual effective dates of the ordinance.
analysis.summary: The provided evidence is a support letter dated June 13, 2022, from various advocacy organizations to the San Jose City Council. It urges the adoption of Item 10.3, a Comprehensive Parking Policy and TDM Ordinance, scheduled for a vote on June 14, 2022. The letter argues that eliminating parking minimums and shifting to market-based parking supply will reduce greenhouse gas emissions, lower housing costs, and support climate neutrality goals.
